text: Description: web-check.xyz is a free and open-source website scanner that performs comprehensive technical analysis of websites. It provides detailed insights on security configurations, DNS records, SSL certificates, server information, performance metrics, and more, all through an easy-to-use web interface.
Category: Reconnaissance, Web Security Assessment, OSINT, Technical SEO
As web-check.xyz is available as a hosted service, no installation is required for basic use.
For hosted service at web-check.xyz, no configuration is required.
Set in .env file in project root
Key variables include API keys for optional third-party services
Can be configured in the server configuration files
Enter a domain name in the search bar and click "Analyze"
Review comprehensive results organized by categories
View detailed explanations of each check
Expandable sections for each analysis category
Detailed technical explanations for each finding
Enter a domain name (e.g., "example.com") in the search field
View comprehensive results across all check categories
Click individual category tabs to focus on specific aspects:
This is how a general scan of Web-check looks like.
This image shows a server location report for Google.com from web-check.xyz. The analysis displays geographical and technical information about the server hosting Google.com.
Languages: en-GB, cy-GB, gd (English-Great Britain, Welsh-Great Britain, Scottish Gaelic)
Server Provider: GOOGLE (as indicated in parentheses)
This image displays SSL certificate information for www.google.com from web-check.xyz, showing the security certificate details that establish an encrypted HTTPS connection.
Issuer: Google Trust Services (truncated in display)
Extended Key Usage: TLS Web Server Authentication
Certificate Validity: The certificate is valid until June 2025, indicating proper maintenance. In red teaming, expired certificates would be a security concern to highlight.
Cryptographic Strength: Uses P-256 curve (NIST) which is currently considered secure. Red teamers should verify appropriate cryptographic standards are in use.
Issuer Verification: Certificate issued by Google Trust Services - a legitimate Certificate Authority. Red teams should verify issuers match expected organizations to detect potential SSL spoofing.
Extended Key Usage: Properly limited to "TLS Web Server Authentication" - more permissive settings could represent unnecessary security exposure.
Recent Renewal: Recently renewed in March 2025, showing active certificate management, which is a positive security practice.
Certificate Transparency: Verify the certificate appears in public CT logs
Domain Validation: Check if certificate covers all relevant subdomains and alternate names
Certificate Chain: Validate the complete trust chain back to a trusted root
Key Strength Analysis: Assess if cryptographic parameters meet current standards
Potential Vulnerabilities: Look for weaknesses like SHA-1 signatures or weak ciphers
Certificate Misconfigurations: Check for missing constraints or overly permissive settings
This image shows WHOIS information for GOOGLE.COM from web-check.xyz, displaying registration details of the domain.
Registered Domain: The domain name being queried (GOOGLE.COM)
Creation Date: When the domain was first registered (15 September 1997)
Updated Date: When the domain information was last modified (9 September 2019)
Registry Expiry Date: When the domain registration will expire if not renewed (14 September 2028)
Registry Domain ID: Unique identifier for the domain in the registry database (2138514_DOMAIN_COM-VRSN)
Registrar WHOIS Server: Server that provides detailed WHOIS information (whois.markmonitor.com)
Registrar: Company through which the domain is registered (MarkMonitor Inc.)
Registrar IANA ID: Internet Assigned Numbers Authority identifier for the registrar (292)
Usage in Security Assessments & Red Teaming
Domain Age Assessment: The creation date (1997) indicates this is a well-established domain, reducing likelihood it's a malicious clone.
Expiration Monitoring: Domains nearing expiration (this one is valid until 2028) could be targeted for domain hijacking if renewal is missed.
Registrar Security: MarkMonitor is an enterprise-grade registrar specializing in brand protection, indicating good security practices.
Historical Analysis: The last update date (2019) might indicate when ownership or important details changed.
Social Engineering Preparation: Understanding domain ownership helps craft believable pretexts for social engineering attacks.
Domain Typosquatting: Identifying older domains helps attackers register similar-looking domains for phishing (e.g., g00gle.com).
Target Validation: During penetration testing, confirming genuine domain ownership prevents testing unauthorized targets.
Corporate Structure Mapping: Domain registration details can reveal parent companies and organizational relationships.
Domain Expiration Attacks: Though not applicable here (expires 2028), domains nearing expiration could be monitored for hijacking opportunities.
OSINT Starting Point: Registrar information can lead to additional records that may reveal technical contacts or infrastructure details.

Defensive Measures: Organizations should regularly audit their domain registrations, use privacy protection services when appropriate, and ensure domains are registered with reputable registrars using secure authentication.
This image shows the HTTP security headers and firewall detection results for a website (likely Google.com based on previous images) from web-check.xyz.
The scan reveals mixed implementation of security headers:
Note: "*The domain may be protected with a proprietary or custom WAF which we were unable to identify automatically"
Without CSP, the site has no browser-enforced restrictions on sources of executable scripts, styles, and other resources
Potential exploitation: Could allow script injection attacks if other vulnerabilities exist
Red team opportunity: Cross-site scripting attacks might be more viable without this protection
Without HSTS, the site doesn't force browsers to always use HTTPS
Potential exploitation: SSL stripping attacks could be possible
Red team opportunity: Man-in-the-middle attacks to downgrade connections
Without this header, browsers might MIME-sniff content types
Potential exploitation: Could lead to MIME confusion attacks
Red team opportunity: Uploading files that bypass content type restrictions
Protects against clickjacking by controlling iframe embedding
Reduces risk of UI redressing attacks
Enables browser's built-in XSS filtering capabilities
Provides some (limited) defense against cross-site scripting
The note about a possible proprietary WAF is significant:
Large organizations often use custom security solutions
Standard fingerprinting techniques may fail to detect these
Red teams should use progressive probing techniques to identify protection mechanisms
Absence of detected WAF doesn't mean absence of protection
Focus on missing headers (CSP, HSTS, X-Content-Type-Options)
Test content injection where CSP would normally prevent execution
Try protocol downgrade attacks in absence of HSTS
Use incremental, low-noise probing to identify WAF patterns
Test with different user agents and request patterns
Consider time-delayed attacks to bypass rate limiting
Test for XSS vulnerabilities despite X-XSS-Protection (as this header is deprecated in modern browsers)
Look for upload functionality that might allow MIME-type confusion
Clearly report missing headers as security findings
Recommend implementation of missing protective headers
Note that modern best practices favor CSP over older headers like X-XSS-Protection
This image displays HTTP response headers from a website (presumably Google.com based on previous images) obtained via web-check.xyz.
expires: 1 January 2001 - Deliberately set in the past to prevent caching
cache-control: 1 January 2000 - Unusual format (should contain directives, not dates)
content-type: text/html; charset=ISO-88... (truncated) - Defines content as HTML with ISO-8859-1 character encoding
content-security-policy-report-only: object-src 'none';base-uri... (truncated) - Policy in "report-only" mode (violations reported but not enforced)
accept-ch: Sec-CH-Prefers-Color-Scheme... (truncated) - Client Hints requesting browser preferences
p3p: CP="This is not a P3P pol... (truncated) - Legacy Privacy Policy header, seemingly using non-standard value
server: gws - Google Web Server identifier
x-xss-protection: 1 January 2000 - Malformed (should be "0" or "1; mode=block"), appears to be a date instead
x-frame-options: SAMEORIGIN - Restricts iframe embedding to same origin only
set-cookie: SOCS=CAAaBgiAouXABg; expi... (truncated) - Setting a cookie with various attributes
alt-svc: h3=":443"; ma=2592000,h3-... (truncated) - Alternative services header, advertising HTTP/3 support
transfer-encoding: chunked - Indicates response is sent in chunks rather than all at once
Date-formatted values in unexpected headers: Several headers (cache-control, x-xss-protection) contain dates instead of standard directives, suggesting:
Might be a fingerprinting opportunity to identify backend systems
CSP in "report-only" mode means policies aren't enforced
x-xss-protection value is non-standard and ineffective
CSP Bypass: With report-only mode, CSP violations are monitored but not blocked, potentially allowing:
Caching Inconsistencies: Multiple conflicting cache headers:
Could lead to browser-specific caching behaviors
Might be leveraged for cache poisoning attacks
Cookie Analysis: The set-cookie header warrants further inspection for:
Server fingerprinting is possible via "gws" identifier
Alt-svc reveals HTTP/3 support and port information
Test server responses to modified header values
Check if malformed headers trigger unexpected behaviors
Since CSP is in report-only mode, test what violations would be allowed
Verify if report endpoints are properly secured
Without HSTS, test for SSL stripping vulnerabilities
This image shows DNS-related information for a domain (likely Google.com based on previous images) from web-check.xyz, displaying both DNS records and DNS server details.

Should normally map to an IPv6 address, but shows an IPv4 address instead (potentially unusual configuration)

Address of the responding DNS server
Reverse DNS name for the IP address
The 1e100.net domain is owned by Google (1e100 = scientific notation for "googol")
DNS over HTTPS is not supported
Note indicates this might be a false negative
Useful for understanding target network topology
Unusual AAAA record (containing IPv4) suggests non-standard DNS configuration
MX record in unusual format may indicate custom mail infrastructure
No DoH (DNS over HTTPS) support means DNS queries are potentially:
The hostname pattern reveals naming convention
Could be used to predict and discover additional infrastructure
IP address can be used to identify network blocks
Helpful for determining logical network boundaries
MX record reveals mail infrastructure details
Could be targeted for email-based attacks
Test if DNS tunneling is detected/blocked
Check if DNS servers allow zone transfers (AXFR)
Could reveal additional subdomains and records
Test susceptibility to DNS spoofing attacks
Verify mail server security based on MX record
Test for common mail server vulnerabilities
Cross-reference IP location with hostname indication (lhr)
May reveal CDN or load balancing infrastructure
This image displays the TLS cipher suites supported by a webserver (likely Google.com based on previous images) from a web-check.xyz scan.
The server supports the following cipher suites:
ECDHE: Elliptic Curve Diffie-Hellman Ephemeral - Provides forward secrecy
Some ciphers lack ECDHE, meaning no forward secrecy
RSA: Used in the first four ciphers for authentication
AES128/256: Advanced Encryption Standard with 128/256-bit keys
DES-CBC3: Triple DES in Cipher Block Chaining mode (legacy)
SHA/SHA256/SHA384: Secure Hash Algorithms for integrity verification
DES-CBC3-SHA (3DES) is notably outdated and considered weak by modern standards
Vulnerable to Sweet32 attack when large amounts of data are transmitted
Red team opportunity: Test for downgrade attacks forcing use of this weaker cipher
Several ciphers lack authenticated encryption (non-GCM variants)
More susceptible to padding oracle and related attacks
Red team opportunity: Test for CBC padding oracle vulnerabilities
Non-ECDHE ciphers don't provide forward secrecy
If server private key is compromised, past communications could be decrypted
Red team opportunity: Document as security finding
Force connection with only weak ciphers (especially DES-CBC3-SHA)
Document if server accepts downgraded connections
Check for TLS 1.0/1.1 support alongside these ciphers
Older TLS versions + weak ciphers compound security issues
Verify if server prioritizes stronger ciphers
Attempt to negotiate less secure options
Check against standards like PCI DSS (which prohibits 3DES)
Test for known TLS vulnerabilities, particularly with CBC mode ciphers
Disable the obsolete DES-CBC3-SHA cipher suite
Ensure only ciphers with forward secrecy (ECDHE) are preferred
Implement a modern cipher suite order that favors strongest options first
Consider implementing additional TLS hardening like HSTS

This means cookies are sent to all subdomains

Missing HttpOnly flag allows JavaScript access
Could be exposed in XSS attacks
Red team opportunity: Target for cookie theft via XSS
Properly implement both Secure and HttpOnly flags
Better protected against common cookie attacks
Allows cookies to be sent on top-level navigations
Red team opportunity: Test for CSRF in specific contexts where Lax doesn't protect
"__Secure-" prefix on ENID cookie enforces HTTPS
Browsers will only accept this cookie over secure connections
Extends potential exploit window if compromised
Red team opportunity: Long-term session persistence if stolen
Focus on the SOCS cookie which lacks HttpOnly protection
Craft proof-of-concept exploits that extract cookie values
Test edge cases where SameSite=Lax doesn't protect
Focus on non-GET requests that might still be vulnerable
Determine cookie purposes through behavior analysis
Identify which cookies are essential for authentication
Test session handling when cookies are modified or partially presented
Test if compromising a subdomain allows access to these cookies
All cookies have the Secure flag, but test edge cases
Check for scenarios where cookies might be sent over HTTP
Note the missing HttpOnly flag on SOCS as a finding
Suggest upgrading SameSite=Lax to Strict where applicable
Recommend shorter expiration times as a best practice
This image shows security.txt file information and HSTS status for a website (Google.com based on previous images) from web-check.xyz.
Security.txt is a proposed standard (RFC 9116) that allows websites to define security policies and contact information for security researchers.
PGP Signed: ❌ No - File is not cryptographically signed
Contact: /vulnz - Relative path to vulnerability reporting page
Expires: 1 April 2030 - Far future expiration date
View Full Policy: Option available to view complete content
HTTP Strict Transport Security (HSTS) is a security feature that helps protect websites against protocol downgrade attacks and cookie hijacking.
Note: "Site does not serve any HSTS headers."
Identifies security program structure including VRP
Red team benefit: Legitimate communication channels to report findings
Missing PGP Signature: File is not cryptographically signed
Cannot verify authenticity of security.txt content
Red team opportunity: Emphasize in findings report
Best practice is 1 year maximum expiration
Red team opportunity: Note as policy non-adherence
No protection against SSL stripping attacks
Cookies potentially exposed to MitM attacks
Red team opportunity: Demonstrate SSL stripping attack
Large organizations often have complex infrastructure making HSTS deployment challenging
May be using alternative protections not detected by scan
Attempt to access all referenced resources (/vulnz, /vrp, etc.)
Test response time to submitted findings
Perform SSL stripping attacks to demonstrate risk
Test cookie security when connection is downgraded
Recommend HSTS implementation with appropriate max-age
Verify the email contact actually works
Check if vulnerability reporting mechanisms are functional
Confirm public key is valid and up-to-date
This image shows DNSSEC (Domain Name System Security Extensions) configuration information for Google.com from web-check.xyz.
DNSSEC is a suite of extensions that add security to the DNS protocol by enabling DNS responses to be validated for authenticity.
DNSKEY Present: ❌ No - DNS Key records are not available
DS Present: ❌ No - Delegation Signer records are not available
RRSIG Present: ❌ No - Resource Record Signature records are not available

DNSKEY records contain the public keys used to verify DNSSEC signatures in RRSIG records. These keys authenticate DNS data and verify that it hasn't been tampered with. The absence means Google.com isn't publishing cryptographic keys for signature verification.

DS records reside in the parent zone and hold the hash of a DNSKEY record in the child zone. They create a chain of trust between the parent zone (com.) and the child zone (google.com). The absence indicates there's no cryptographic linkage establishing trust between these zones.

RRSIG records contain digital signatures for DNS record sets. They provide authentication and integrity protection for DNS records. The absence means DNS responses for Google.com aren't cryptographically signed.
DNSSEC Non-Implementation: Google.com operates without DNSSEC protection, relying on alternative security measures.
DNS Trust Model: Reveals Google's security strategy may focus on endpoint security rather than DNS-layer protections.
DNS Spoofing Vulnerability: Without DNSSEC, the domain remains theoretically vulnerable to DNS cache poisoning and spoofing attacks.
Man-in-the-Middle Potential: Attackers could potentially intercept and modify DNS responses to redirect users to malicious sites.
Trust Chain Fragility: No cryptographic chain of trust exists between the TLD (.com) and Google's authoritative nameservers.
DNS Cache Poisoning: A red team could potentially demonstrate DNS poisoning attacks against recursive resolvers serving Google.com.
BGP Hijacking + DNS Attack: Combine route hijacking with DNS spoofing to redirect traffic from legitimate Google services.
Local Network Attacks: On compromised networks, demonstrate DNS response manipulation to redirect Google traffic.
DNS Spoofing Simulation: Set up controlled environments to demonstrate the impact of successful DNS spoofing.
Resolver Security Testing: Evaluate how different DNS resolvers handle potentially spoofed Google.com responses.
Split-Horizon DNS Tests: Test if internal vs. external DNS configurations differ in vulnerability.
HTTPS Bypass Attempts: Test if DNS spoofing combined with SSL stripping could affect users despite HTTPS.
Risk Contextualization: Major companies like Google often make calculated decisions about DNSSEC implementation based on:
Alternative security measures (HTTPS, HSTS, CAA records, Certificate Transparency)
Remediation Strategy: While reporting the absence of DNSSEC, a red team should:
Acknowledge the nuanced risk calculation for high-traffic domains
Demonstrate practical attack scenarios that specifically affect the target
Suggest a phased DNSSEC implementation approach if warranted
Implement DNSSEC: Deploy DNSSEC signing for critical domains to prevent DNS spoofing.
DNS Monitoring: Establish monitoring for unexpected DNS changes or anomalies.
DNS-over-HTTPS/TLS: Consider supplementing with encrypted DNS protocols.
DANE Records: Implement TLSA records to bind certificates to DNS (requires DNSSEC).

Google's non-implementation of DNSSEC isn't necessarily an oversight—many large organizations evaluate the complexity, performance impacts, and alternative protections before deciding on DNSSEC deployment. The absence should be evaluated alongside their overall security architecture.
TLS Security Issues Analysis for Google.com
This image shows TLS (Transport Layer Security) configuration analysis for Google.com from web-check.xyz, highlighting various security issues and recommendations.
Host: google.com - The target domain being analyzed
CA Authorization: ✅ Yes - CAA records are properly implemented
CAAs allowed to Issue Certs: pki.goog - Only Google's internal PKI is authorized to issue certificates
Mozilla Grading: 91.5 - Relatively high security rating based on Mozilla's SSL configuration standards
No distrusted Symantec SSL?: ✅ Yes - Site is not using problematic Symantec certificates that were distrusted industry-wide
Certificate Rank: 1 October 2001 - Likely indicates when the root certificate was created/trusted
Mozilla Evaluation Level: non compliant - Does not fully comply with Mozilla's modern security standards
These are issues that affect compatibility with older systems while maintaining security:
SHA256WithRSAEncryption issue: "SHA256WithRSAEncryption is not an old certificate signature, use sha1WithRSAEncr..." - Suggests using older signature algorithm for compatibility
Missing Ciphers: "Consider adding ciphers ECDHE-ECDSA-CHACHA20-POLY1305, ECDHE-RSA-CHACHA20-POLY13..." - Recommends additional cipher suites for compatibility
Missing Protocol: "Add protocols SSLv3" - Suggests adding an older, now deprecated protocol
OCSP Stapling: "Consider enabling OCSP stapling" - OCSP stapling isn't enabled but would improve performance
Certificate Type: "Use a certificate of the type rsa, not ECDSA" - Recommends RSA over ECDSA for broader compatibility
Issues that affect systems not using the latest standards:
Missing Ciphers: Similar cipher recommendation as above for ChaCha20-Poly1305
OCSP Stapling: Same recommendation as above
Certificate Type: Same recommendation to use RSA over ECDSA
Issues that affect security for modern systems:
Remove Weak Ciphersuites: "Remove ciphersuites ECDHE-RSA-AES128-SHA, ECDHE-RSA-AES256-SHA, AES128-GCM-SHA25..." - Recommends removing older, potentially vulnerable cipher suites
Add Strong Ciphers: "Consider adding ciphers ECDHE-ECDSA-AES384, ECDHE-ECDSA-CHACHA20-POLY..." - Suggests stronger modern ciphers
Remove Old Protocols: "Remove protocols TLSv1, TLSv1.1" - Recommends removing older TLS versions
OCSP Stapling: Same recommendation as previous sections
Certificate Authority Details: Google uses its internal PKI (pki.goog) for certificate issuance, indicating a custom CA infrastructure
Implementation Priorities: Google seems to prioritize wide compatibility over strict adherence to the newest standards
Security vs. Compatibility Balance: Despite scoring 91.5, Mozilla considers it "non-compliant," suggesting calculated trade-offs
Potential support for older TLS versions (TLSv1, TLSv1.1) increases attack surface
Missing modern cipher suites may reduce perfect forward secrecy protections
Lack of OCSP stapling affects certificate revocation checking efficiency
Some supported ciphers use older algorithms potentially more vulnerable to attacks
Missing ChaCha20-Poly1305 ciphers that would be more efficient on mobile devices
Configuration prioritizes compatibility over strictest security
ECDSA vs RSA certificate choice affects compatibility with older clients
OCSP stapling not implemented, potentially allowing privacy leakage during certificate validation
If TLSv1/TLSv1.1 are indeed supported, potential for forcing connections to use older protocols
Test if server can be forced to use weaker ciphers through client manipulation
The specific cipher configuration creates a unique fingerprint for Google's infrastructure
Useful for identifying Google-owned properties or impersonation detection
Test OCSP checking behavior - without stapling, clients might skip revocation checking if OCSP responders are slow
Test certificate validation behavior with various client configurations
Verify if older TLS versions are actually negotiable
Document potential security impacts if downgrades succeed
Test which ciphers are actually negotiated with different client configurations
Identify any preference for weaker ciphers that could be exploited
Test how Google handles certificate revocation
Evaluate performance impact of missing OCSP stapling
Verify that the CAA record is correctly limiting certificate issuance to pki.goog
Test if any loopholes exist in CAA implementation
Google's choices likely reflect calculated decisions balancing security, performance and compatibility
High-traffic sites often make different security trade-offs than smaller sites
The Mozilla score of 91.5 indicates generally strong security despite "non-compliant" status
Document how these choices affect older clients and browsers
Analyze the potential user base that benefits from these compatibility choices
Disable TLSv1 and TLSv1.1 if still enabled
Implement TLSv1.3 if not already in place
Add recommended ChaCha20-Poly1305 ciphers for better mobile performance
Remove older cipher suites that are no longer considered secure
Implement OCSP stapling to improve certificate validation performance and privacy
Consider certificate type recommendations based on client analysis
Monitor for emerging TLS vulnerabilities and best practices

For large organizations like Google, TLS configuration decisions often involve complex trade-offs between security, global accessibility, performance, and backward compatibility. Their configurations may intentionally deviate from standard recommendations to support specific business requirements or legacy clients. A red team assessment should recognize these nuances while still highlighting potential security improvements.
TLS Handshake Simulation Analysis for Google.com
This image shows the TLS Handshake Simulation results for Google.com from web-check.xyz, testing compatibility with a wide range of clients.

The TLS Handshake Simulation tests whether Google.com's TLS configuration can successfully establish secure connections with various client devices, browsers, crawlers, and libraries. The scan simulates handshakes from different client systems to verify compatibility.

The image shows extensive compatibility testing results, with nearly all clients successfully connecting (marked with green checkmarks ✅). Only a few specific older clients show failures (marked with red X ❌).
Almost Universal Compatibility: Google.com's TLS configuration successfully establishes connections with:
All major browsers (Chrome, Firefox, Safari, Edge, Opera) across multiple operating systems
All search engine crawlers (Googlebot, Bingbot, Yahoo Slurp) except one older YandexBot version
Breadth of Compatibility: Google's TLS configuration is designed for maximum compatibility, suggesting business prioritization of accessibility over strictest security.
Legacy Support: Supporting very old clients (Android 2.3.7, OpenSSL 0.9.8y) indicates deliberate effort to maintain backward compatibility.
Browser Coverage: Supports all major browsers across multiple operating systems and versions, suggesting extensive testing practices.
Bot Compatibility: Functions with all major search engine crawlers, indicating SEO optimization priorities.
Supporting older clients likely means Google is allowing older TLS protocol versions (TLS 1.0, TLS 1.1)
This creates potential downgrade attack vectors where attackers could force connections to use older, more vulnerable protocol versions
Wide compatibility requires supporting older cipher suites
Some older supported ciphers may have known cryptographic weaknesses
Supporting Android 2.3.7 (released in 2011) and other legacy systems exposes the site to potential exploitation via deprecated cryptographic methods
Older clients typically lack modern security mitigations, making them vulnerable entry points
Google has drawn a security boundary at IE6 and YandexBot 3.0, possibly indicating specific security concerns with these clients
Potential to force connections to use older TLS versions with known vulnerabilities
Example: POODLE attack against TLS 1.0 connections
Man-in-the-middle attackers could potentially force selection of weaker ciphers supported for compatibility reasons
Test for CBC mode ciphers vulnerable to padding oracle attacks
Impersonating older clients could potentially bypass certain security controls
Malicious actors could simulate legacy crawlers or browsers to access content with reduced security measures
Testing with obscure or custom client fingerprints not included in this test suite may reveal additional compatibility or security issues
Verify which TLS versions are actually negotiated with each client type
Test for ability to force downgrade to TLS 1.0 in various network conditions
Document security implications of supported protocol versions
Enumerate all cipher suites accepted from each client category
Identify specific weak ciphers being offered to legacy clients
Test cipher negotiation behavior under various network conditions
Create custom client simulations beyond those in the standard test suite
Test with deliberately malformed client hello messages
Analyze server behavior with unusual or unexpected extensions
Test certificate validation handling with various client capabilities
Verify how clients handle certificate errors or revocation checking
Google's configuration shows a calculated risk management approach, accepting potential security trade-offs for universal compatibility
Appropriate for global platforms with diverse user bases
Provides complete documentation of supported client technologies
Helps trace the minimum security baseline across the client ecosystem
Shows Google's investment in supporting older client technologies
Indicates potential technical debt in security infrastructure
Consider phasing out support for TLS 1.0 and 1.1 for non-critical clients
Apply stricter requirements for sensitive operations (login, payments)
Implement adaptive security based on client capabilities
Provide stronger protection mechanisms for modern clients while maintaining compatibility for legacy systems
Set up monitoring for connection attempts using deprecated protocols/ciphers
Configure alerts for unusual patterns in TLS negotiation
Document a roadmap for incrementally improving TLS security without breaking compatibility
Establish metrics for acceptable compatibility vs. security improvements

This comprehensive compatibility reflects Google's global reach and business imperative to be accessible to all users worldwide, including those on legacy devices and in regions with older technology. The deliberate choice to maintain such broad compatibility represents a calculated business decision that balances security with accessibility.

When conducting red team assessments against similar high-traffic sites, it's important to recognize that these compatibility decisions aren't oversights but strategic choices to serve business requirements. Security recommendations should acknowledge these constraints while presenting risk-based improvement options.
The image displays a "Block Lists" scan result for google.com using web-check.xyz. The scan checks the target domain against multiple security and content filtering services.
Total Block Lists Checked: 19 Status: Not blocked on any list (All entries show "✓ Not Blocked")
Block Lists: These are DNS-based filtering services that categorize and potentially block domains based on security risks, content type, or other criteria:
Security-focused lists (CleanBrowsing Security, Comodo Secure, Quad9): Target malware, phishing, and known threat domains
Family/Content filtering lists (AdGuard Family, CleanBrowsing Family/Adult, CloudFlare Family, Norton Family, OpenDNS Family, Yandex Family): Block adult content and inappropriate websites
General DNS providers (Google DNS, OpenDNS, CloudFlare): May include security filtering options
Status indicators: The green checkmarks with "Not Blocked" text indicate the domain is considered safe/legitimate by each service.
Reputation Analysis: This scan provides visibility into how security services perceive your target domain. For legitimate sites like google.com, no blocks are expected.
False Positive Detection: If your legitimate domain appears on block lists, it may indicate reputation issues requiring remediation.
Security Posture Evaluation: Understanding which security services might block your assets helps identify potential defensive measures in place.
Domains that appear on security block lists will face reduced effectiveness for phishing/social engineering
Consider using newly registered domains or compromised legitimate domains with clean reputations
If targeting organizations using specific DNS filtering (e.g., OpenDNS), knowing block list status helps determine if infrastructure might be filtered
Consider using less-monitored TLDs or subdomain techniques to bypass filtering
Domains not on block lists appear more legitimate to targets and security tools
Test multiple domains to find those with optimal security posture assessments
Check client domains against these lists to identify security gaps
Recommend DNS filtering solutions based on identified weaknesses
Use clean domains as redirectors or C2 infrastructure
Prioritize domains that pass all block list checks for critical infrastructure
Organizations not using DNS filtering solutions are more vulnerable to malicious domains
Target networks using weaker DNS filtering based on block list coverage
Host payloads on domains with clean reputations across major security providers
Use recently expired domains that retain positive reputation
Regularly check your red team infrastructure against these lists
Rotate compromised infrastructure when it starts appearing on block lists

The scan identifies 9 internal links within the google.com domain:
The scan shows 9 external links to other Google properties:
Understanding the Fields for Red Teaming
Internal Link Count: Number of links pointing to pages within the same domain
External Link Count: Number of links pointing to external domains
Internal Links: List of relative URLs within the same domain
External Links: List of absolute URLs pointing to other domains
Each link represents a potential entry point or functionality to probe
Internal links reveal the site's directory structure and key functionality
External links show trusted relationships with other services
URL patterns expose naming conventions and directory structure
Identify key application components through URL paths (e.g., /advanced_search, /services/)
Parameter Analysis: URLs with parameters (like ?hl=en&tab=wn) may be susceptible to:
Open redirects via manipulation of URL parameters (e.g., continue= in login URL)
Path Traversal: Test for directory traversal vulnerabilities in relative paths
Access Control: Test if restricted functionality can be accessed through direct URL navigation
Login URL structure can be used in credential harvesting campaigns
Understanding the site's navigation flow helps create more convincing phishing scenarios
Analyze how parameters in login URLs (ServiceLogin?) handle authentication state
Test for session fixation vulnerabilities in authentication flows
Broken Link Exploitation: Test if any links lead to deprecated or unmaintained pages
Parameter Fuzzing: Systematically test parameters in external links for injection vulnerabilities
Subdomain Enumeration: Use patterns from links to discover additional subdomains
Open Redirect Testing: Focus on links with continue= parameters
Content Discovery: Use identified URL patterns to find similar but potentially unprotected resources
HTTP vs HTTPS: Note that one link (history/optout) uses HTTP rather than HTTPS, potentially indicating legacy code that might have less security scrutiny
The UK-specific domains (.co.uk) reveal geographical targeting and infrastructure distribution
Parameter patterns (e.g., ?tab=w1) suggest a common navigation system across Google properties
The image displays a "Pages" scan result from web-check.xyz showing sitemap indexes found on Google.com.
Key Finding: The site returns a sitemap index, which contains a list of multiple sitemaps across various Google services and products.
Understanding the Fields for Red Teaming
Pages/Sitemap Index: Lists available sitemap files on the target domain
The sitemap files provide structured information about pages and content organization
Sitemaps provide a comprehensive view of the site's architecture without manual crawling
Each sitemap reveals the structure and endpoints of a specific Google service
Unveils potentially less-known services and features (e.g., /nonprofits/, /partners/about/)
Service Enumeration: Identify active Google services and products
Path Disclosure: Reveals directory structures that might not be linked from main pages
Content Mapping: Quickly identify areas of interest for specific attack vectors
Outdated Content: Older or less maintained services might have security gaps

Understanding the Fields for Red Teaming
Open Ports: Ports accepting connections on the target system
Unable to establish connections to: Ports that are either closed, filtered by firewalls, or not responding
The minimal open port footprint (only HTTP/HTTPS) indicates a hardened security posture
Standard web ports suggest the target is primarily a web service with limited additional entry points
The lack of administrative ports (SSH:22, Telnet:23) indicates proper security practices
No database ports (3306/MySQL), mail ports (25/SMTP, 110/POP3, 143/IMAP), or remote access ports (3389/RDP) are accessible
Edge security is likely configured to only allow web traffic
Web-Based Attacks: Focus exclusively on web application vulnerabilities since other service vectors are unavailable:
Test if HTTP/HTTPS ports can be used for tunneling other protocols
Check for HTTP CONNECT method support that might allow proxy pivoting
The limited port exposure suggests a sophisticated edge security model
Look for less hardened assets on the same network using other discovery methods
Consider cloud infrastructure mapping since the target likely uses cloud providers with robust edge security
Standard scanning likely won't reveal additional ports due to:
Potential IDS/IPS systems detecting port scans
Consider low-and-slow scanning techniques or targeted service probing
Protocol Analysis: Test for protocol-specific vulnerabilities on open ports
Load Balancer Detection: Different responses on ports 80/443 might indicate load balancers
CDN Bypass: Attempt to reach origin servers directly, bypassing edge protection
This limited port exposure is expected for a major web service like Google
The proper lockdown of all non-essential ports demonstrates strong security practices
The absence of development/debugging ports (3000, 8000, 8080) shows proper separation of environments

When red teaming against this target, focus entirely on web application attacks, subdomain enumeration, and content discovery since the traditional network attack surface is minimal. Look for misconfigurations in web servers, CDN settings, or application logic rather than attempting to exploit network services.
